perturbation.original_text: BEE Online shall only provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request . The MD will acknowledge receipt of request by email within 2 days and will be responsible for the release of the information within 7 days. Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS or required by Law, B EE Online shall notify the Measured Enterprise within 2 days of receiving the written request by email and will disclose the information within 7 days after Measured Enterprise has been notified BEE Online
perturbation.changed_text: BEE Online shall only provide information about its activities to SANAS or the DTI on request . The MD will acknowledge receipt of request by email within 5 days and will be responsible for the release of the information within 1 day. Before submitting a Measured Enterprise’s Confidential Information when requested by DTI, SANAS or required by Law, B EE Online shall notify the Measured Enterprise within 5 days of receiving the written request by email and will disclose the information within 1 day after Measured Enterprise has been notified BEE Online
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe for acknowledging receipt and releasing information to 5 days and 1 day respectively introduces a contradiction as it may not provide sufficient time to comply with regulations regarding confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 51 of POPIA requires that organizations have reasonable measures in place to protect personal information and to respond to requests for access to information. A 1-day period to disclose confidential information after notifying the Measured Enterprise may be insufficient to ensure compliance with POPIA and other regulations depending on the scope and sensitivity of the data involved and the internal review processes required to ensure data protection and minimize risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for both acknowledging receipt and releasing information, potentially conflicting with the requirements of data protection regulations such as POPIA, as outlined in the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation suggests that a 1-day period may be insufficient to ensure compliance, especially considering the need for internal review processes to minimize the risk of unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to remedy such breach within seven days after receiving written notice from the other party (the “aggrieved party”) requiring it to do so, then the aggrieved party shall be entitled, without prejudice to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled at common law or in equity including inter alia injunction and specific performance. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary cont ained in this agreement, neither party shall be entitled to cancel this agreement in any circumstances whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Should either party (the “defaulting party”) breach any provision of this agreement and fail to remedy such breach within one day after receiving written notice from the other party (the “aggrieved party”) requiring it to do so, then the aggrieved party shall be entitled, without prejudice to the aggrieved party’s other rights in law, to claim from the defaulting party such damages to which it may be entitled at common law or in equity including inter alia injunction and specific performance. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary cont ained in this agreement, neither party shall be entitled to cancel this agreement in any circumstances whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the remedy period to one day introduces a legal contradiction, as it may be considered an unreasonably short time to rectify certain breaches of contract, potentially violating principles of fairness and good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Principle of Good Faith
perturbation.law_citation: South African Contract Law - Principle of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law includes a principle of good faith which imposes an obligation to act honestly and fairly in contractual dealings. A one-day remedy period may be viewed as conflicting with the principle of good faith if it prevents a party from reasonably addressing and remedying a breach, particularly for complex or technical issues, rendering the clause potentially unenforceable.

Code , and Canadian jurisprudence in both Québec and the common law provinces pertaining to contractual and pre-contractual negotiation v t e South African contract law is a modernised form of Roman-Dutch law rooted in canon and Roman legal traditions. [ 1 ] It governs agreements between two or more parties who intend to create legally enforceable obligations. This legal framework supports private enterprise in South Africa by ensuring agreements are upheld and, if necessary, enforced, while promoting fair dealing. Influenced by English law and shaped by the Constitution of South Africa , contract law balances freedom of contract with public policy considerations, such as fairness and constitutional values. Nature [ edit ] A contract is an agreement between parties intended to create legal obligations. A contract in South Africa is an agreement that creates enforceable obligations, distinct from agreements that discharge obligations (e.g., release), transfer rights (e.g., cession), or govern family matters (e.g., marriage settlements). [ 2 ] Contracts are classified as obligat
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the remedy period from seven days to one day. The Law Explanation indicates this could conflict with the principle of good faith under South African contract law because it might not allow sufficient time to address a breach, particularly for complex issues. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly define 'good faith' or remedy periods, snippet 2 mentions that South African contract law promotes fair dealing and is influenced by the Constitution of South Africa, balancing freedom of contract with public policy considerations, such as fairness, potentially supporting the argument that a one-day remedy period is unreasonably short and thus contradicts the principle of fairness implicit in contract law.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may request in writing at any time that any confidential information or any documents containing such confidential information, disclosed pursuant to the terms of this agreement and any copies thereof be returned with a written statement to the effect that upon such return the receiving party has not knowingly r etained in its possession or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such confidential information or any documents containing such confidential information or any copies thereof and the receiving party shall comply with any such request with in seven days of receipt of such request.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may request in writing at any time that any confidential information or any documents containing such confidential information, disclosed pursuant to the terms of this agreement and any copies thereof be returned with a written statement to the effect that upon such return the receiving party has not knowingly r etained in its possession or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such confidential information or any documents containing such confidential information or any copies thereof and the receiving party shall comply with any such request with in thirty days of receipt of such request.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the return period to 30 days could violate certain data protection regulations, particularly where expedited data removal or destruction is legally mandated upon request.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 79 of POPIA outlines conditions for direct marketing. It requires organisations to cease processing personal information for direct marketing purposes upon request. Waiting 30 days to return or destroy confidential information, particularly personal data used for marketing, can be deemed non-compliant with the requirement to cease processing data as soon as reasonably practicable.
perturbation.justification: The change from a 7-day to a 30-day compliance period introduces an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction. The law explanation argues that a 30-day delay may conflict with the POPIA requirement to cease processing personal information for direct marketing purposes as soon as reasonably practicable. The Perturbation Explanation also states that extending the return period could violate data protection regulations, where expedited data removal is legally mandated upon request. Although there are no scraped snippets, the change in time is enough to create a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice given by a party to the other – 12.2.1 Delivered by hand during the normal business hours of the addressee at the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received by the recipient at the time of delivery. 12.2.2 Posted by prepaid registered post from an address within the Republic of South Africa to the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received by the recipient on the fourth day after the date of posting.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice given by a party to the other – 12.2.1 Delivered by hand during the normal business hours of the addressee at the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received by the recipient at the time of delivery. 12.2.2 Posted by prepaid registered post from an address within the Republic of South Africa to the addressee’s domicilium for the time being shall be rebuttably presumed to have been received by the recipient on the tenth day after the date of posting.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the presumption of receipt for registered post to ten days is inconsistent with typical legal standards and postal service efficiency in South Africa.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African law of evidence regarding postal service delivery presumption.
perturbation.law_citation: Law of Evidence Amendment Act 45 of 1988
perturbation.law_explanation: While the specific Law of Evidence Amendment Act 45 of 1988 does not explicitly define the presumed delivery time for registered post, South African courts generally consider a reasonable period. A ten-day presumption may be viewed as unduly long in the context of domestic postal services and could be challenged as unreasonable and inconsistent with established legal practices regarding the rebuttable presumption of delivery. The links provided offer guidance on the application of the laws of evidence in South African courts and the context for rebuttable presumptions.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulates a four-day presumption for receipt of registered post, while the changed text extends this to ten days. The perturbation explanation asserts that this extension is inconsistent with legal standards and postal service efficiency in South Africa. Although specific legal snippets were not provided to definitively establish a legally mandated timeframe, the law explanation suggests that South African courts expect a reasonable period for domestic postal services and that a ten-day presumption might be viewed as unduly long and inconsistent with established legal practices. Given that the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type covers scenarios where time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements and the changed text establishes a timeframe that is likely unenforceable due to unreasonableness, a contradiction exists.
